title: Isaiah 25
subtitle: The Feast on the Mountain: Death Swallowed Up

heb.paragraph: "LORD, you are my God; I will exalt you and praise your name, for in perfect faithfulness you have done wonderful things (peleʾ), things planned long ago" (v.1). After ch.24’s devastation, a hymn of praise. God has made the city a heap of rubble; the foreigners’ stronghold is no more (v.2). Ruthless nations will honour God; the poor and needy find shelter in the storm (vv.3–4). The blast of the ruthless is like a storm against a wall, like heat in the desert — but God silences the uproar (v.5).

notes.note: "In perfect faithfulness you have done wonderful things, planned long ago." God’s works are both wonderful and ancient. Nothing is improvised; everything was planned.
"You have been a refuge for the poor, a shelter from the storm, a shade from the heat." The three images: refuge, shelter, shade. Each addresses a different danger: poverty, violence, oppression.

refs.note: 'He will wipe every tear from their eyes' — John quotes Isaiah 25:8 directly for the new creation.
'Death has been swallowed up in victory' — Paul quotes Isaiah 25:8 for resurrection hope.
The wedding feast of the king — Jesus' parable draws on Isaiah's banquet imagery.
The marriage supper of the Lamb — the ultimate fulfillment of Isaiah's mountain feast.

hist.context: The feast on Mount Zion (vv.6-8) is one of the most sublime images in prophetic literature. 'Aged wine,' 'rich food,' 'the best of meats' — this is no ascetic vision but lavish celebration. The 'shroud' and 'sheet' covering all peoples (v.7) likely refers to mourning cloth; God removes the pall of death from all nations. This universalism is remarkable: the feast is 'for all peoples.' The chapter may have liturgical origins, perhaps used in temple worship anticipating God's final victory.

notes.note: Oswalt reads the opening hymn as response to chapter 24's judgment: what God has done to the ruthless city evokes praise from the poor and needy who find refuge in him.

The centerpiece: God hosts a feast for all nations on his holy mountain. Oswalt emphasizes 'all peoples' (repeated) — this is not Israel alone but universal salvation. Death itself is 'swallowed up' — not merely postponed but abolished.

Childs notes this passage functions as the theological center of the Isaiah Apocalypse. The reversal is complete: from cosmic catastrophe (ch.24) to cosmic celebration (ch.25). Judgment serves salvation.

The New Testament citations (1 Cor 15:54; Rev 21:4) show this verse became foundational for Christian eschatology. Childs reads it as the Old Testament's clearest statement that God's final purpose is life, not death.

sections.header: Verses 6–12 — “He Will Swallow Up Death Forever”

heb.gloss: he will swallow up / he will destroy
heb.paragraph: "He will swallow up death (billaʿ) forever. The Sovereign LORD will wipe away the tears from all faces" (v.8). THE mountain-feast and THE death-of-death. "On this mountain the LORD Almighty will prepare a feast of rich food for all peoples, a banquet of aged wine — the best of meats and the finest of wines" (v.6). The feast is for ALL PEOPLES. On this mountain he will destroy the shroud that enfolds all peoples, the sheet that covers all nations (v.7) — the veil of death. Then v.8: death SWALLOWED. Paul quotes this in 1 Corinthians 15:54: "Death has been swallowed up in victory." Tears wiped: Revelation 21:4 quotes this. "The disgrace of his people he will remove from all the earth" (v.8). "In that day they will say, ‘Surely this is our God; we trusted in him, and he saved us. Let us rejoice and be glad in his salvation’" (v.9).

notes.note: "On this mountain the LORD will prepare a feast of rich food for all peoples." The eschatological banquet: Jesus’ parables of the wedding feast (Matt 22:1–14; Luke 14:15–24) develop this. The feast is on the mountain; the guests are ALL peoples.

"He will destroy the shroud that enfolds all peoples, the sheet that covers all nations." Death is a burial shroud covering every nation. God tears it off. The universality: ALL peoples, ALL nations are under the shroud; ALL are liberated.

"He will swallow up death forever. The LORD will wipe away the tears from all faces." Two of the most quoted OT verses in the NT: 1 Cor 15:54 (death swallowed) and Rev 21:4 (tears wiped). The Old Testament’s most explicit promise of death’s destruction.
"Surely this is our God; we trusted in him, and he saved us." The response: recognition. The God they trusted turns out to be exactly who they believed he was. Trust vindicated. Salvation received.

refs.note: Moab and Edom as archetypal enemies — Obadiah's oracle against Edom parallels Isaiah's judgment on proud nations.
Moab's pride mentioned in the earlier Moab oracle — a consistent theme in Isaiah's treatment of this neighbor.
Zephaniah also condemns Moab's arrogance and taunting of God's people.

hist.context: Moab appears as the representative proud nation trampled in the 'dung heap' (v.10). This crude imagery emphasizes total humiliation. Moab's history with Israel was complex: Ruth was Moabite, yet Moab often opposed Israel. The contrast between Zion's feast (vv.6-8) and Moab's humiliation (vv.10-12) illustrates the reversal theme: those who exalt themselves are brought low; the humble are lifted up.

notes.note: Oswalt highlights the confession: 'This is our God, we trusted in him.' The feast-goers are those who waited and hoped. Their patience is vindicated; their faith rewarded.
The Moab oracle seems jarring after the universal feast. Oswalt reads it as representing all who refuse the invitation — pride excludes itself from grace.

Childs sees the Moab section as deliberately contrasting with the feast: the same mountain that hosts salvation also witnesses judgment. The chapter holds both together: God's 'yes' and 'no' are not contradictory but complementary.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: "On this mountain the LORD will prepare a feast of rich food for all peoples…

ppl.name: nations
ppl.text: "He will destroy the shroud that enfolds all peoples, the sheet that covers all nations…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVOn this mountain he will destroy the shroud that enfolds all peoples, the sheet that covers all nations;ESVAnd he will swallow up on this mountain the covering that is cast over all peoples, the veil that is spread over all nations.NIVIn that day they will say, “Surely this is our God; we trusted in him, and he saved us. This is the Lord, we trusted in him; let us rejoice and be glad in his salvation.”ESVIt will be said on that day, “Behold, this is our God; we have waited for him, that he might save us. This is the LORD; we have waited for him; let us be glad and rejoice in his salvation.”

rec.text: ""In perfect faithfulness you have done wonderful things, planned long ago." God’s works are both wonderful and ancient. Nothing is improvised; everything was planned."

rows.label: Hymn of Praise — 25:1–5
rows.text: "Wonderful things, planned long ago." Refuge for the poor. Storm silenced.

hebtext.note: "LORD, you are my God; I will exalt you and praise your name, for in perfect faithfulness you have done wonderful things (peleʾ), things planned long ago" (v.1). After ch.24’s devastation, a hymn of praise. God has made the city a heap of rubble; the foreigners’ stronghold is no more (v.2). Ruthless nations will honour God; the poor and needy find shelter in the storm (vv.3–4). The blast of the ruthless is like a storm against a wall, like heat in the desert — but God silences the uproar (v.5).

hebtext.gloss: he will swallow up / he will destroy
hebtext.note: "He will swallow up death (billaʿ) forever. The Sovereign LORD will wipe away the tears from all faces" (v.8). THE mountain-feast and THE death-of-death. "On this mountain the LORD Almighty will prepare a feast of rich food for all peoples, a banquet of aged wine — the best of meats and the finest of wines" (v.6). The feast is for ALL PEOPLES. On this mountain he will destroy the shroud that enfolds all peoples, the sheet that covers all nations (v.7) — the veil of death. Then v.8: death SWALLOWED. Paul quotes this in 1 Corinthians 15:54: "Death has been swallowed up in victory." Tears wiped: Revelation 21:4 quotes this. "The disgrace of his people he will remove from all the earth" (v.8). "In that day they will say, ‘Surely this is our God; we trusted in him, and he saved us. Let us rejoice and be glad in his salvation’" (v.9).

themes.note: Mercy AND Prophecy both peak at 10: death swallowed (1 Cor 15:54), tears wiped (Rev 21:4), feast for all peoples (Matt 22). The OT’s most explicit death-destruction promise. Mission: ALL peoples, ALL nations.

coaching.tip: The praise in verses 1–5 responds to the cosmic judgment of chapter 24. Isaiah alternates between terror and worship — the pattern of apocalyptic literature. Judgment is not the final word; doxology is. The literary structure embodies the theology: God's destruction of the proud elicits not despair but gratitude from those who see it rightly.

'He will swallow up death forever' (v. 8) — Paul quotes this in 1 Corinthians 15:54 as the climax of his resurrection argument. In Isaiah's context, this is the banquet on God's mountain where tears are wiped away (v. 8, echoed in Revelation 21:4). One verse in a minor chapter of Isaiah generates two of the New Testament's most powerful images. Death's defeat and tears' removal start here.
